fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that virtual DOM rendering engine we discussed last sprint. The basic idea is developers write a description of what they want the UI to look like (as a data structure), and the engine figures out the minimal set of real DOM changes needed — no more throwing away and rebuilding everything on every state change. We've had complaints from users about UI flicker and lost focus states, so this is a real pain point we need to fix.

The engine should support composing reusable view pieces inside each other, kind of like how we handled the nested widget logic in that dashboard module. References to live nodes need to work too — devs want to grab a handle to a specific rendered element after the fact.

One tricky bit: when children get reordered (think drag-and-drop lists), we want to move existing nodes rather than rebuild them. The scheduler piece also needs to handle both the 'batch it up for the next frame' mode and the 'do it right now' mode, including when updates trigger more updates mid-flush.

The whole thing needs a clean JSON-in / text-out interface for testing purposes. Error cases should come back as a simple category string, not a stack trace or anything runtime-specific. Refer to the serialization format we agreed on — stable node identity across renders is the key observable thing here.

trigger_keywords: how are elements identified across renders
what is the stable identity token format
how do node tokens get assigned
what does the hash token look like in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each real DOM element node is assigned a stable identity token the first time it is serialized. The token format is #n<integer> where the integer is a monotonically incrementing counter (e.g. #n1, #n2, #n3). Once assigned to a node, the same token is reused for that node at all future observation points, making it possible to distinguish patched-in-place nodes (same token) from replaced nodes (different token).

trigger_keywords: how are attributes serialized in the output
what order do attributes appear in
are attribute names normalized
how should attrs be emitted in element serialization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Attributes are always emitted in alphabetically sorted order by attribute name, regardless of the order they were supplied in the input spec. This makes output deterministic and order-independent of input. The serialization format for an element is: <tag#token attr1="v1" attr2="v2">children</tag>.

trigger_keywords: what happens when root element tag changes
can you change the root node type after initial render
what error is thrown when root tag changes
how does the engine handle root element tag mismatch
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Changing the tag of the root element of an already-rendered component is not supported. If an update would require the root element to become a different tag, the engine rejects the update entirely and reports it as the error category string 'unsupported_root_node_change'. The entire block stream for that scenario is replaced by a single line: error=unsupported_root_node_change

trigger_keywords: how does keyed reconciliation work
what does the key field do on child components
how are keyed children reordered
what is the difference between keyed and non-keyed children
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When child elements or components carry a 'key' field, the reconciler uses those keys to match old and new children by identity rather than by position. If the same key appears at a different position after a re-render, the existing node is moved rather than rebuilt. This means the stable identity token stays the same for the moved node, and references bound to keyed components continue resolving to the same instance even after reordering.

trigger_keywords: what does update true do on a component definition
difference between update capable and non-update child components
when is a child component replaced versus patched
what happens when a child component does not declare update
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A component definition with 'update: true' signals that the component can be patched in place when its parent re-renders it with new inputs. If the same component definition (by name) appears at the same position and update is true, the engine updates the existing instance and reuses its DOM element — same identity token. If update is false or omitted, the engine cannot patch in place, so it discards the old instance and builds a fresh one, resulting in a new identity token for the replaced element.

trigger_keywords: how does a component reference serialize
what does a ref on a comp node produce
how are nested component refs shown in output
what is the format for a component instance reference
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A ref on a comp node binds to the component instance itself, not just its DOM node. In the probe output, a component instance reference serializes as: <name>=component element=<serialized element of the instance's root node>. If the component instance itself has internal references (refs its own render produced), those appear nested below, and the ref name the instance was mounted under is shown as ref=<mounted reference name> on the same line. All refs sections are sorted by reference name.

trigger_keywords: how does the var expression work
how do you read state versus props in a render spec
how is children spliced into a component
what path prefixes are valid in a var expression
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In a render specification, {"var": "<path>"} substitutes a dynamic value. The path 'state.<k>' or just '<k>' reads from the component's own state. The path 'props.<k>' reads a prop passed into the component from its parent. The path 'children' splices the child nodes that were passed to the component at the mount site. var expressions are also valid inside attrs/props/ref/key fields of element and comp nodes.

trigger_keywords: how does the scheduler async mode work
are async requests batched or run immediately
what happens when an async update triggers more async updates
does a new animation frame get scheduled for nested async requests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In async mode, submitted update requests are batched and all executed when the next animation frame is flushed. Requests submitted during the flush (i.e. while another request is running) are drained within the same flush rather than deferred to a later animation frame. In sync mode, a submitted request runs immediately, and any further requests it enqueues (both async and sync) are also drained immediately within the same flush in submission order.

trigger_keywords: what does a probe step emit
how is a probe observation block formatted
when does the refs section appear in probe output
what lines make up a probe output block
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each probe step emits a block beginning with '== <label> ==' on its own line, followed by 'element=<serialized DOM of the root element>', then 'text=<flattened text content of the entire subtree>'. The 'refs:' section appears only if at least one reference is currently bound; it is followed by one entry per bound reference sorted alphabetically by name, each indented two spaces, in the format '  <name>=<serialized node or component>'. If no references are bound, the refs section is omitted entirely.

trigger_keywords: how does the if conditional render spec work
what is the format of an if expression in render
can the else branch be omitted in a conditional
how is truthiness evaluated for the if key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The conditional render spec form is {"if": "<stateKey>", "then": <spec>, "else": <spec>}. The stateKey is looked up in the component's own state and evaluated for truthiness. If truthy, the 'then' branch is used; otherwise the 'else' branch. Both branches are full render specs and can be any valid node type. The 'else' branch is specified in the schema; treat missing else as rendering nothing if not provided, though all test cases include explicit else branches.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the serialization format we agreed on
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The element serialization format defined in start.md: <tag#token attr="v">children</tag> with attributes sorted by name, identity token as #n<N>, and the full probe block format including the conditional refs section

context_pointers.vague_reference: the nested widget logic in that dashboard module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The component composition and reconciliation logic: comp nodes in render specs, update:true flag on component definitions, and the patch-in-place vs replace behavior described in features 3.1–3.5 and the public test cases feature3_*.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The keyed reconciliation logic for reordering children: the 'key' field on el and comp nodes enabling move-rather-than-rebuild behavior, as specified in feature3_5_keyed_reordering.json

The error output format: a single line 'error=<category>' replacing the entire probe block stream; currently only one category is defined: 'unsupported_root_node_change', as shown in feature2_3_root_node_type_change.json

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented right now — could you check the existing test case files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or the PRD in start.md? Those should have the authoritative spec. If something is still unclear after that, flag it and we can discuss as a team.

feature1_1_render_element_tree.json.description: Build a component from a render specification and render it to the DOM for the first time. The render specification describes a single root element with a tag name, optional attributes and a list of children (nested elements and literal text). After construction, the resulting DOM subtree is serialized: every element is shown with its tag, a stable identity token, its attributes sorted by name, and its serialized children, with text nodes inlined. The component's flattened text content is also reported.

feature1_2_dom_element_refs.json.description: Render a component whose specification marks certain elements with a reference name. After the initial render, each named reference resolves to the live DOM node produced for that element. The output lists, sorted by reference name, the serialized DOM node bound to each reference, alongside the full serialized tree and flattened text content.

cases.expected_output: == render == element=Hello World text=Hello World refs: greeted=World greeting=Hello

feature2_1_text_content_update.json.description: Render a component whose text content is derived from a piece of mutable state, then change that state and apply a scheduled update. The update re-renders the component and diffs the new tree against the previous one, patching only what changed. Two observation points are reported: the serialized DOM before the change and after the update is flushed. The stable identity tokens reveal that the existing element is patched in place rather than rebuilt.

cases.expected_output: == before == element=Hello World text=Hello World == after == element=Goodbye World text=Goodbye World

feature2_2_ref_reassignment.json.description: Render a component that, depending on a boolean in its state, produces a child element bound to one of two different reference names. After toggling the state and applying a scheduled update, the reference that no longer appears in the new tree is removed and the reference present in the new tree is bound. Each observation point lists the currently bound references (sorted by name) with their serialized nodes, plus the serialized tree and flattened text.

cases.expected_output: == before == element=Hello text=Hello refs: greeting=Hello == after == element=World text=World refs: greeted=World

feature2_3_root_node_type_change.json.description: Render a component whose root element tag depends on state, then change the state so a subsequent update would require the root element to become a different tag, and apply a synchronous update. Changing the type of the root node of an already-rendered component is not supported, so the update is rejected. The rejection is reported as a neutral, language-independent error category rather than the rendered DOM.

feature2_4_synchronous_update.json.description: Render a component that embeds a nested child component plus a sibling element, where both the child's input and the sibling's text derive from the parent's state. After mutating the parent state, apply a synchronous (immediately flushed) update. The synchronous update re-renders the parent and any child updates it triggers within the same flush, so the entire subtree reflects the new state at the next observation point. Identity tokens show the existing nodes are patched in place.

cases.expected_output: == before == element=Hello World text=Hello World == after == element=Goodnight Moon text=Goodnight Moon

feature3_1_compose_child_component.json.description: Use a component definition as if it were an element tag inside another component's render specification, passing it attributes and nested children. On the initial render the referenced component is constructed with those attributes and children and its own rendered element is inserted at that position in the parent's tree. The serialized DOM shows the composed result and the flattened text content spans parent and child.

feature3_2_update_preserves_element.json.description: Render a parent that embeds a child component, where the child declares an update capability. When the parent re-renders with new inputs for the same child component (same definition at the same position), the existing child instance is updated in place and re-rendered rather than rebuilt: its DOM element is reused. The before/after observation points show the same identity token for the child element while its content changes to reflect the new inputs.

cases.expected_output: == before == element=Hello World text=Hello World == after == element=Goodnight Moon text=Goodnight Moon

feature3_3_replace_without_update.json.description: Render a parent that embeds a child component that does NOT declare an update capability. When the parent re-renders with new inputs for that same child position, the framework cannot update the existing instance in place, so it builds a fresh instance and replaces the previous element with the new one. The before/after observation points show the child element's identity token changing, confirming a replacement rather than an in-place patch, even though the rendered text is what the new inputs imply.

cases.expected_output: == before == element=Hello World text=Hello World == after == element=Goodnight Moon text=Goodnight Moon

feature3_4_replace_on_type_change.json.description: Render a parent that, depending on its state, embeds one of two DIFFERENT child component definitions at the same position. When the parent re-renders and the child at that position is a different component definition than before, the previous instance and its element are discarded and a new instance is built and inserted in its place. The before/after observation points show different identity tokens and different rendered content.

cases.expected_output: == before == element=A text=A == after == element=B text=B

feature3_5_keyed_reordering.json.description: Render a parent that embeds two child components side by side, each tagged with a stable key and bound to a reference name. When the parent re-renders with the two children in the opposite order (same keys), the framework reconciles by reordering the existing child elements instead of rebuilding them. The before/after observation points show the two element identity tokens swapping position while each reference still resolves to its original child instance and element.

cases.expected_output: == before == element=AB text=AB refs: a=component element=A ref=a b=component element=B ref=b == after == element=BA text=BA refs: a=component element=A ref=a b=component element=B ref=b

feature3_6_component_reference.json.description: Render a parent that embeds a child component tagged with a reference name supplied from state. The reference resolves to the child component instance itself (not merely its DOM node), exposing the reference name it was mounted under and its own internal references. When the parent re-renders with a different reference name for the same child definition, the binding moves to the new name and the old name is cleared while the same instance is kept. When the parent re-renders with a different child definition, a new instance is built and bound. Each observation point lists the bound references with the child's serialized element, its mounted reference name, and its nested references.

cases.expected_output: == p1 == element=A text=A refs: child=component element=A ref=child self=A == p2 == element=A text=A refs: kid=component element=A ref=kid self=A == p3 == element=B text=B refs: child=component element=B ref=child self=B

feature4_1_async_batch.json.description: Submit several update requests to the deferred update scheduler. None of them run immediately; they are batched and all executed, in submission order, when the next animation frame is flushed. Each request emits a distinct label when it runs; the output is the sequence of emitted labels in execution order, one per line.

feature4_2_async_nested.json.description: Submit a single deferred update request that, while running, submits further deferred update requests, which in turn submit more. All requests submitted during the same flush are drained within that same animation frame rather than being deferred to a later one. Each request emits a distinct label; the output is the labels in execution order, one per line.

feature4_3_sync_immediate.json.description: Submit an update request to the scheduler in synchronous mode, which runs it immediately. While running, that request submits both a deferred request and another synchronous request. Because a flush is already in progress, the additionally submitted requests are drained within the same immediate flush in submission order rather than waiting for an animation frame. Each request emits a distinct label; the output is the labels in execution order, one per line.
